Final Step: Narrowing Down To Just Five Companies

In this step, we construct three separate lists of five stocks each, with different sets of goals, dividend income, and risk levels.

The lists are:

1) Relatively Safe (Low-yield) Dividend list,

2) Moderately High Dividend List,

3) Ultra High Dividend List, and

4) A combined list of the above three (duplicates removed).

Out of the top 40 (41 this time), we make our judgment calls to make these three lists, so basically, the selections are based on our research and perceptions. So, while most of the filtering until now was based on automated criteria, the last step is pretty much subjective. We try to make each of the three lists highly diversified among various sectors and industry segments, and try to ensure that the safety of dividends matches the overall risk profile of the group. We certainly encourage readers to do further research on the highlighted names.

Nonetheless, here are our three final lists for this month:

Final A-List (Relatively Safe Income):

Criteria for this group:

  • The average yield for the group is between 2.75% and 4.25%.
  • At least three or four names with 'A' rated.
  • Solid track record of dividends and growth.

Average yield: 3.64%

  • (INTU)
  • (NEE)
  • (MS)
  • (CVX)
  • (UPS)

Table-1A: A-LIST (Conservative Income)

We think this set of five companies (in the A-List) would form a solid diversified group of dividend companies that would be appealing to income-seeking and conservative investors, including retirees and near-retirees. The average yield is 3.64%, which is more than 3x of what the S&P 500 pays. The average dividend history is 22 years. Also, all five companies have an excellent rating of 'A-' or higher. If you must need higher dividends, consider the B-List or the C-List, presented later.

As usual, we will provide a brief synopsis of each of the companies selected here; however, we will not go into too many details, as that will make the article unduly long.

INTU (Intuit Inc):

The current dividend yield of Intuit is very low, but the dividend growth has been close to 20% over the last five and 10-year periods. TurboTax and QuickBooks are its two major premium products leading the charge. So, this is included here primarily for the dividend growth, which should also lead to share price gains.

Intuit appears to be firing on all cylinders and has achieved 20% revenue growth in the fourth quarter and 16% growth on a full-year basis, both beating management's own guidance. The operating margins have also been expanding nicely. There are some risks and competitive threats, such as the IRS’s free “Direct File” program. However, it poses minimal risk to Intuit's business as the software is very basic and can address only simple returns. The program also offers no support for state taxes, which impacts customers in 41 states.

NEE (NextEra Energy):

NEE is a well-run Utility company and has grown its dividend for the last 31 years. In that sense, it is also a member of the so-called dividend aristocrats. NEE is one of the best companies in the Utility sector, and its energy business is a leader in clean and renewable energy.

Its current forward dividend yield of 3.15% is nearly 40% above its 5-year average yield of 2.25%. The share prices have come down significantly from the highs of 2024. That said, the share prices have stayed in a tight range since the beginning of 2025. We feel the stock is an attractive and relatively safe investment. Also, Morningstar rates it fairly priced.

Since utility companies carry relatively high debt, the high-interest-rate environment impacts their valuations adversely. However, it is widely expected now for the rates to come down gradually. So, when the interest rates do come down, the stock price is likely to gain significantly. In the meantime, investors can earn relatively safe dividends.

CVX (CHEVRON):

We think CVX is one of the best investments in its sector for mid-level and stable income. However, earnings and the share price of the company are obviously tied to the movement of the oil prices, which can fluctuate widely based on geopolitical situations of the day. That being said, the company provides relative safety, an excellent credit rating, stable business, and a reasonably high dividend yield for a large company.

Similar to previous months, we are including CVX in the A-list for reasons stated above. CVX currently offers a highly attractive dividend yield of close to 4.3%. Chevron is known to be a low-cost producer with its breakeven price in the low to mid-40s. The payout ratio is in the low 60s on an EPS basis and only in the 40s on a cash flow basis. The dividend growth has been a bit tepid over the years, but reasonable at 6% plus considering the starting yield. The company maintains an excellent credit rating of 'AA' from S&P, which shows its financial strength.

MS (Morgan Stanley):

Morgan Stanley has been scoring well in our ratings since last year. Since then, the price has moved up significantly, along with the broader market. So, from a valuation point of view, the company is no longer attractive. Morningstar rates the share prices as roughly 15% overvalued. For this reason, it should only be accumulated on a dollar-cost-average basis.

Other than valuation, we see many positives with the company. It is one of the largest investment banks when you count its revenue. It has roughly $5 trillion in clients' assets, with a majority of it under the supervision of their financial advisors. The past 5-year and 10-year dividend growth has been exceptionally good at over 21% and 23%, respectively, and the payout ratio is still very reasonable at 42%. However, we are unlikely to see in the future the kind of dividend growth we witnessed in the past 10 years. The future growth is likely to be much more moderate between 7% to 10%. For example, the annual dividend increases in the last two years have been 9% and 8%.

UPS (United Parcel Service):

UPS is one of three triopolies or major multinationals in the logistics sector (the other two being FedEx and DHL). However, the company’s share price has lost nearly 37% of its value during the last 52 weeks. That’s not a pretty picture. Due to falling share price, the dividend yield has become very attractive and currently is roughly 7.5%. Also, the payout ratio is uncomfortably high at over 80%. The negative sentiment is a combination of many factors. First, it is in the process of ramping down the Amazon package business. Secondly, the tariff-related concerns, especially with the expiry of the tariff exemption on de-minimis packages, have added to the woes. Thirdly, the agreement negotiated by UPS with its employee unions in 2023 is proving to be expensive and driving wage inflation. The company is in the midst of downsizing and cost-cutting initiatives; however, it is yet to be seen how successful it will be. On the positive side, UPS maintains a solid balance sheet and maintains an excellent credit rating of 'A' from the S&P. Its debt is relatively high but manageable. As per Morningstar, the share price is nearly 25% below its fair value. The current yield of 7.5% is more than double of its long-term 5-year average of 3.08%. Even though the company has termed the dividend as “rock-solid” in the recent earnings call, we will only know this in the future. In a nutshell, the company is a turnaround story right now. Patient investors are likely to be rewarded as the shares appear to be at the bottom, but the execution risk remains. This is the only high-risk, high-reward stock we have included in the A-list.

Final B-List (High Yield, Moderately Safe):

Criteria for this group:

  • Yield for the group between 4.75% to 6.25%
  • At least two names with 'A-' (or above) rated.
  • Above-average track record of dividends and growth

Average yield: 6.30%

  • (NEE)
  • (TGT)
  • (MPLX)
  • (UPS)
  • (ARCC)

Note 1: Very often, we include a few low-risk stocks in the B-list and C-list. Also, oftentimes, a stock can appear in multiple lists. This is done on purpose. We try to make each of our lists fairly diversified among different sectors/industry segments of the economy. We try to include a few of the highly conservative names in the high-yield list to make the overall group much safer.

Table-1B: B-LIST (High Yield)

NOTE: MPLX is structured as a partnership (not a corporation) and issues a K-1 tax form instead of the usual 1099-Div. Please use due diligence.

In the B-List, the overall risk profile of the group becomes slightly elevated compared to the A-List. That said, the group (as a whole) will likely provide safe dividends for many years. This list offers an average yield for the group of 6.30% and an average of 27 years of dividend history.

Some of our high-yield selections often repeat in the B-list and C-list.

MPLX (MPLX LP):

For the B-List and C-List, we are replacing Chevron with MPLX for the energy sector, mainly to elevate the yield. Please note that the company has a Partnership structure and issues a K-1 tax form (partnership income) instead of the usual 1099 in case of regular dividends.

We have covered MPLX multiple times in our previous articles in this monthly series. Hence, we will skip the details here. MPLX regularly features on our B-list and C-list due to its stable dividend growth and high yield. There are other good alternatives, such as Enterprise Products Partners (EPD) or Energy Transfer (ET).

ARCC (Ares Capital):

We are including Ares Capital in the B-list and the C-list. ARCC is one of the largest BDCs (Business Development Companies) in terms of market capitalization and net asset value. The company invests in small and medium-sized companies and has a diversified portfolio of investments in over 500 companies. Besides, ARCC has a proven record of performing reasonably well both in high and low-interest rate environments. Pricing and valuation-wise, it trades at a slight premium for being one of the largest in the BDC arena, and its stable dividend.

It generates a solid amount of NII (net Investment income), which currently exceeds well above the $1.2 billion mark on an annual basis. ARCC currently offers an attractive yield of roughly 8.3%.

Final C-LIST (Yield-Hungry, Less Safe):

Criteria for this group:

  • The average yield for the group is between 7.0% to 8.0%
  • All names with investment-grade ratings.
  • Some potential for growth.

Average yield: 7.29%

  • (NEE)
  • (MPLX)
  • (UPS)
  • (LYB)
  • (ARCC)

Notes:

Note 1: Oftentimes, a stock can appear in multiple lists. We try to include one or two conservative names in the high-yield list to make the overall group much safer.

Table-1C: C-LIST (Yield-Hungry, Elevated Risk)

NOTE: MPLX is structured as a partnership (not a corporation) and issues a K-1 tax form instead of the usual 1099-Div. Please use due diligence.

=====

Apparently, this list (C-List) is for yield-hungry DGI investors. The yield goes up to 7.3%. However, this list is not for conservative investors. As you can see, the average credit rating of this set of companies is much lower than the A-List or even the B-List. Dividends are moderately safe in this list, but the yields are very attractive. At least a couple of companies in this list come with an elevated level of risk. We urge due diligence to determine if it would suit your personal situation. Nothing comes for free, so there will be more risk involved with this group. That said, it's a highly diversified group spread among five different industry segments.

LYB (LyondellBasell Industries, N.V.):

LyondellBasell is one of the prominent companies in the materials sector and appears to be undervalued. It has been trading at attractive levels for some time, but the prices have undergone further correction due to the expectation of a global economic slowdown. The current yield is very tempting at nearly 9.7%. Please note that there is a high risk that the company comes under further pressure and cuts its dividends, which it has avoided steadfastly so far (unlike its peers). Considering these risks, only patient and risk-tolerant investors should consider it.

We are including LYB in our C-list because of the high yield and undervaluation. Morningstar currently assigns a four-star valuation rating to LYB. Morningstar's own fair-value estimates for LYB have come down in the last year; even then, the current market price is roughly 37% below their estimates.

LYB deals in petrochemicals and commodity chemicals and has a large manufacturing presence in North America, which provides it access to relatively cheaper natural-gas-based feedstocks even though it is headquartered in the Netherlands. The petrochemicals sector has been facing some cyclical headwinds in general, as well as pricing pressures from new production capacity in Asia.

Note: We would like to caution that each company comes with certain risks and concerns. Sometimes, these risks are real, but other times, they may be a bit overblown and temporary. So, it's always recommended to do further research and due diligence.

What If We Were to Combine the Three Lists?

If we combine the three lists and remove the duplicates (because of combining), we would be left with nine names.

Two-time duplicates: ARCC and MPLX (2 duplicates).

Three-time duplicates: NEE and UPS (4 duplicates)

After removing these SIX duplicates, we are left with nine names.

The combined list is highly diversified in as many as eight different industry segments. However, there may be multiple names for some of the sectors. The stats for the group of 9 are as follows:

Average yield: 5.42%

Average discount (from 52WK High): -17.6%

Average 5-yr dividend growth: 9.5%

Average 10-yr dividend growth: 10.2% (from 8 names)

Average Payout Ratio: 53.9%

Average Total Quality Score: 69.4

Table 2:

Conclusion:

In this article, we have presented three groups of dividend-paying stocks (five each) with different goals in mind to suit the varying needs of a wider audience. Even though the risk profile of each group is different, each group in itself is fairly balanced and diversified.

This month, the first group yields 3.64%, while the second group elevates the yield to 6.30%. We also presented a C-list for yield-hungry investors with a 7.29% yield. The combined group (all three lists combined with duplication removed) offers an even more diversified group with nine positions and a 5.4% yield.

data.title: Weekly Indicators: Neutral Initial Claims Complement Abysmal Employment Report
data.description:
Purpose

I look at the high frequency weekly indicators because while they can be very noisy, they provide a good nowcast of the economy, and will telegraph the maintenance or change in the economy well before monthly or quarterly data is available. They are also an excellent way to "mark your beliefs to market." In general, I go in order of long leading indicators, then short leading indicators, then coincident indicators.

A Note on Methodology

Data is presented in a "just the facts, ma'am" format with a minimum of commentary so that bias is minimized.

Where relevant, I include 12-month highs and lows in the data in parentheses to the right. All data taken from St. Louis FRED unless otherwise linked.

A few items (e.g., Financial Conditions indexes, regional Fed indexes, stock prices, the yield curve) have their own metrics based on long-term studies of their behavior.

Where data is seasonally adjusted, generally it is scored positively if it is within the top 1/3 of that range, negative in the bottom 1/3, and neutral in between. Where it is not seasonally adjusted, and there are seasonal issues, waiting for the YoY change to change sign will lag the turning point. Thus I make use of a convention: data is scored neutral if it is less than 1/2 as positive/negative as at its 12-month extreme.

With long leading indicators, which by definition turn at least 12 months before a turning point in the economy as a whole, there is an additional rule: data is automatically negative if, during an expansion, it has not made a new peak in the past year, with the sole exception that it is scored neutral if it is moving in the right direction and is close to making a new high.

For all series where a graph is available, I have provided a link to where the relevant graph can be found.

Recap of Monthly Reports

August data started out with sharp rebounds in the ISM manufacturing and services indexes, but an abysmal employment report that was recessionary in all ways except the headline number. In particular, *all but one* of the leading indicators in the report were negative.

July Construction spending was also negative, as were factory orders.

Long leading indicators

Interest rates and credit spreads

Rates

  • BAA corporate bond index 5.97%, unchanged w/w (1-yr range: 5.33-6.43)
  • 10-year Treasury bonds 4.09%, down -0.14% w/w (3.66-4.98)
  • Credit spread 1.88%, up +0.14% w/w (1.36-2.02).

(Graph at Moody's Seasoned Baa Corporate Bond Yield | FRED | St. Louis Fed.)

Yield curve

  • 10 year minus 2 year: +0.56%, down -0.05% w/w (-1.07 - +0.53)
  • 10 year minus 3 month: +0.08%, unchanged w/w (-1.65 - 0.17)
  • 2 year minus Fed funds: -0.80%, up +0.09% w/w.

(Graph at 10-Year Treasury Constant Maturity Minus 2-Year Treasury Constant Maturity | FRED | St. Louis Fed.)

30-Year conventional mortgage rate (from Mortgage News Daily)

  • 6.29%, down -0.31% w/w (6.11-7.26).

Treasury and corporate bond yields, as well as mortgage rates, remain lower than their near 12 month highs of a number of months ago, but the trend is mainly sideways. The spreads between Treasuries and investment grade and high yield bonds continue to show little change, having meandered in a relatively narrow range for most of the past several years.

The yield curve measures remain where they have been, more or less, for months, although with a deeper “bow” than recently, with the 10-2 year spread being the most positive, and the 2 year vs. Fed funds being the most negative. The 10 year minus 3 month remained neutral again this week.

Housing

Mortgage applications (from the Mortgage Bankers Association)

  • Purchase apps down -3% to 147 (114-163) [SA]
  • Purchase apps 4 wk avg. unchanged at 149 [SA]
  • Purchase apps YoY% up +17% [NSA]
  • Purchase apps YoY 4 wk avg. +20.5% [NSA]
  • Refi apps down -4% w/w [SA]
  • Refi apps YoY up +19% [SA].

*[SA] = seasonally adjusted, [NSA] = not seasonally adjusted.

Real Estate Loans (from the FRB)

  • Down -0.1% w/w
  • Up +1.4% YoY.

(Graph at Real Estate Loans, All Commercial Banks | FRED | St. Louis Fed.)

Both purchase mortgage and refinancing applications have been volatile, but within a limited range over the past 18 months. Both are now substantially higher YoY, but remain close to their longer term bottoms, so they score neutral.

In 2023 real estate loans sank below 1/2 of their 12 month high, the last housing indicator to turn negative, and have generally continued to worsen..

Money supply

The Federal Reserve has discontinued this weekly series. Data is now only released monthly. July data was just released last week:

  • M1 m/m up +0.3%, YoY Real M1 up +1.8% (3 year high) (worst: -1.4% 8/23).
  • M2 m/m up +0.4%, YoY Real M2 up +2.0% (2.1% June 2025 3 year high) (worst -8.6% 9/23).

No recession has happened without a YoY real M1 negative, or YoY real M2 below +2.5%. Real M2 fell below that threshold in March 2022. Real M1 also turned negative as of May 2022. In the past, when real money supply has rebounded to the levels it is at now, it is either during the latter part of a recession, or in the period of recovery, so both of these are positive long leading metrics now.

Corporate profits (Q2 actual, Q3 estimated) from I/B/E/S via FactSet at p. 27)

  • Q2 up +0.63 to 67.02, up +5.3% q/q
  • Q3 at 67.62, up +0.9% q/q.

FactSet estimates earnings, which are replaced by actual earnings as they are reported, and are updated weekly. The "neutral" band is +/-3%. I also average the previous two quarters together, until at least 100 companies have actually reported. In Q1 earnings wound up lower by more than -3%, which turned the rating negative. But in Q2 earnings rebounded sharply, causing the rating change to positive.

Credit conditions (from the Chicago Fed)

  • Financial Conditions Index up +.03 (less loose) to -0.53 (-0.42 - -0.67)
  • Adjusted Index (removing background economic conditions) up +0.02 (less loose) to -0.50 (-0.34 - -0.61)
  • Leverage subindex unchanged (loose) at -0.41 (+0.13 - -0.76).

In these indexes, lower = better for the economy. The Chicago Fed's Adjusted Index's real break-even point is roughly -0.25. In the leverage index, a negative number is good, a positive poor. The historical breakeven point has been -0.5 for the unadjusted Index. After briefly deteriorating to neutral several months ago, all three had been solidly positive, but the unadjusted index remains neutral.

Short leading indicators

Economic Indicators from the late Jeff Miller’s “Weighing the Week Ahead”

  • Miller Score (formerly "C-Score"): down -9 to 377, down -27 m/m (237 9/13/24 - 421 7/25/25) Components: Yield curve 55 Phillips curve 331
  • St. Louis Fed Financial Stress Index: up +0.0378 to -0.7126 (+0.5569 4/11/25 - -.9773 1/24/25)
  • BCIp from Georg Vrba: up +9.8 to +55.7 as of 9/4/25 iM's Business Cycle Index (100 is max value, below 25 is recession signal averaging 20 weeks ahead).

The Miller Score is designed to look 52 weeks ahead for whether or not a recession is possible. Any score over 500 means no recession. This number fell below that threshold at the beginning of August 2021, so not only is it negative, but we are now years into the “recession eligible” time period. This indicator is heavily influenced by both the yield curve and the still very low unemployment rate vs. gas prices (expecting a reversal) which explains why it remains negative. My colleague who compiles this score began disaggregating the two components several months ago.

The St. Louis Financial Stress index is one where a negative score is a positive for the economy, and during its limited existence, has risen above zero before a recession by less than one year. It remained very positive until briefly in April. It then recovered and remains among its better post-pandemic readings.

For almost 18 months IM has stated that their system does not forecast a recession, although it did recently edge closer to their threshold.

Trade weighted US$

  • Down -0.09 to 120.60 w/w, down -0.9% YoY (last week) (broad) (119.67 - 130.21) (Graph at Nominal Broad U.S. Dollar Index.
  • Up +0.02 to 97.79 w/w, down -3.4% YoY (major currencies) (graph at link) (99.01- 109.66).

The neutral range for the US$ is between unchanged to 5% higher YoY. At 5% higher or more YoY, the strength suppresses exports and incentivizes imports, a negative for the economy. The trend in these measures had been from below 0 (thus positive) to neutral, to above 5% (negative) for 18 months, until the beginning of this year. Both measures are lower YoY now, so are positives.

A reminder that I mechanically score a slowly depreciating US$ as a positive because it makes exports cheaper and imports more expensive, thereby assisting domestic production.

Commodity prices

Bloomberg Commodity Index

  • Down -0.36 to 102.43 (93.30 - 107.71)
  • Up +9.3% YoY.

(Graph at https://www.marketwatch.com/investing/index/bcom?countrycode=xx.)

Bloomberg Industrial metals ETF (from Bloomberg) (graph at link)

  • 141.82, down -0.73 w/w (130.47 -173.08).
  • Up +2.8% YoY.

This spring the industrial measure gradually weakened from positive to negative. Then the broad commodity index declined sharply into negative territory, but then rebounded into neutral range and now positive. By contrast, the industrial metals measure remained very negative. This can be attributed to whether or not oil is included among the commodities tracked. Last week the industrial measure turned back higher YoY, and so positive. Please note, importantly, that if commodities were not priced in US$ terms, but in other major currencies, it is a completely different story. In particular, that industrial commodities are weaker YoY, despite a weaker US$, is definitely not a sign of global strength.

Stock prices S&P 500 (from CNBC) (graph at link)

  • Up +0.3% to 6481.50.

In July and again this month, stocks have made several new all-time highs. As a result this indicator is positive.

Regional Fed New Orders Indexes

(*indicates report this week) (no reports this week)

  • Empire State, up +13.4 to 15.4
  • Philly down -20.5 to -1.9
  • Richmond up +19 to -6
  • Kansas City up +3 to +5
  • Dallas up +9.4 to +5.8
  • Month-over-month rolling average: up +6 to +4.

The regional average is more volatile than the ISM manufacturing index, but usually correctly forecasts its month-over-month direction. Since spring 2022, these gradually declined to neutral and then negative. In the past 45 days, these have improved to neutral and, if the average increases just +1 more, to +3, this will score as a positive.

Since May I have added the regional service indexes. Those reports posted in the past week noted with an *:

  • Empire State down -2.4 to -11.7
  • Philly up +7.2 to -10.3
  • Richmond up +14 to +6
  • Kansas City up +6 to +4
  • Dallas up +4.8 to +6.8
  • Average: up +3 to -1.

The biggest surprise in all of the high frequency data this month has been the sharp rebound in manufacturing all the way to solidly positive. Meanwhile the regional non-manufacturing reports, which had stabilized at negative levels since May, have rebounded all the way to neutral.

Employment metrics

Initial jobless claims

  • 237,000, up 8,000 w/w, up 3.9% YoY
  • 4-week average 231,000, up 2,500 w/w, up 0.4% YoY.

(Graph at St. Louis FRED.)

Jobless claims were positive for most of 2024, except for hurricane-influenced weeks during the autumn. Then they turned moderately negative through midyear this year. But in the past seven weeks, and five weeks ago this indicator has improved to positive. This may be an issue with unresolved seasonality accounting for educational layoffs and rehiring, but it could also be a reflection of non-native born workers in certain industries being deported or being afraid to show up for work, meaning remaining crews are much more tightly held. This particular issue may have been resolved with the higher YoY readings this week.

Temporary staffing index (from the American Staffing Association) (graph at link)

  • Unchanged at 88 w/w
  • Down -1.2% YoY (low -13.6%- high -1.5% 8/7/25).

This series has been negative since early 2023. YoY comparisons continued to be negative throughout 2024. It remains frankly recessionary. I suspect this has been a secular change and giving a false signal as a result. Relatively speaking, the YoY reading has been trending “less bad” for the past number of months, and finally, since it is now more than half the way to unchanged on a YoY basis, to neutral as of earlier this year.

Tax Withholding (from the Department of the Treasury)

  • $276.2 B for the month of August vs. $261.1 B one year ago, up +$15.1 B or +5.8%.
  • $268.8 B for the last 20 reporting days vs. $251.5 B last year, up +$17.3 B or +6.9%.

These were very positive for almost all of 2024, and with the exception of seven weeks (four after an April 15 anomaly), the trend has continued this year.

Oil prices and usage (from the E.I.A.)

  • Oil down -$1.97 to $62.01 w/w, down -7.0% YoY ($58.21 - $83.88).
  • Gas prices up +.03 to $3.18 w/w, down -$0.11 YoY
  • Usage 4-week average down -0.8% YoY.

(Information can be found at “This Week In Petroleum” at the E.I.A.’s website.)

Oil prices first declined into the bottom 1/3rd of their 3 year range, and so turned positive late in 2024, and then declined even further earlier this year. After the Israeli-Iran hostilities last month, oil prices increased, but have since receded back to close to four year lows.

Gas prices also recently made new 3 year lows, and so are also positive. Last year, mileage driven had been generally negative. It turned neutral and then positive earlier this year, but negative again last week. Since March of this year it has oscillated between positive and negative.

Note: given this measure’s extreme volatility, I believe the best measure is against their 3 year average.

Bank lending rates

  • 4.41% Secured Overnight Financing Rate (SOFR), up +0.09% w/w.

The TED Spread has been discontinued, and as of last September so was LIBOR. At the suggestion of a reader, I began to track the SOFR instead. Unfortunately, SOFR has only been in existence since 2018, so there is no track record has to how it might behave around normal recessions (vs. the pandemic). Over the past 5 years, it does appear to have matched the trend in LIBOR. Given the alternatives, I am now including it in my list of indicators in the conclusion.

Coincident indicators

Tariff payments (from the Department of the Treasury)

  • July 2025 vs. July 2024: $29.6 B vs. $8.7 B (+$20.9 B)
  • August 2025 vs. August 2024: $31.4 B vs. $8.9 B (+$22.5 B).

I have inaugurated this measure temporarily since other sources, such as inferences from the CPI and PPI, are imprecise and speculative so far. The above information comes directly from the Daily Treasury Statement. These fees are either absorbed by the importer or passed on to the consumer, or some proportion of each, and are removed from potential consumption on other items. Note that the above payments probably include very little from the further tariff hikes that went into effect last month.

St. Louis FRED Weekly Economic Index

  • Down -0.08 to 2.52 w/w (Low 1.65 November 2, 2024 - high 3.21 November 23, 2024).

This measure was in the neutral range during most of early 2024, but generally positive (above 2.00) since then, and came very close to setting a new 12 month high three months ago, before declining. After four weeks back in neutral range, including a new 6 month low, several months ago it returned to positive territory.

Restaurant reservations YoY (from Open Table) State of the Restaurant Industry |

  • Last week - 7 day average up +9% YoY, vs. +10% one week prior.

Since 2023 this index has been very volatile, swinging between positive, neutral, and negative several times. In general earlier this year the YoY trends improved, but with a great deal of noise. In the past two months, however, this metric trended sharply higher, before backing off somewhat in the last two weeks.

Consumer spending

  • Johnson Redbook up +6.5% YoY, 4 week average +6.1% (high 7.4% 4/25/25; 4.8% 2/1 and 7/3, 2025) United States Redbook Index.

The Redbook index has been positive since February last year. It had very good readings in April and early May, probably due to consumers front-running anticipated tariff price increases and shortages. Afterward it had been weakening - until six weeks ago, when it stabilized. Three weeks ago it increased sharply, and has remained very positive since. I suspect this means that the period of backlash from previous front-running of the tariffs has ended.

Consumer inflation by Truflation (Independent, economic & financial data in real time on-chain | Truflation)

  • Down -0.22% to +1.93 YoY (High 3.11% 12/11/24 - Low 1.06% 9/13/24).

This recent addition is a daily update to inflation, similar to the “billion prices project” of the last decade (which required a subscription). I have not added this to my list below of coincident or leading indicators, but needless to say it is an up-to-the-moment reading on this very important indicator. This series has been volatile, and the YoY comparisons had been turning higher since September, but has been very low in the last several months, although it did make another new two month high three months ago.

Real Consumer Spending

  • Up +4.6% YoY (high - 6.0% 4/25/25; 12 month low 1.7%% 12/16/24).

This metric premiered at the beginning of last year. One of my most important mantras is that consumption leads employment. Real retail sales have a long history of doing so, but are only reported on a monthly basis.

The weekly result is derived simply by subtracting YoY inflation as measured by Truflation from the YoY change in nominal consumer spending as measured by Redbook. While it will be somewhat noisy, it should anticipate changes in the monthly measures ahead of time. It backed off from the 12 month high it set in February, and then gradually rebounded, and made another 12 month high several months ago. After mainly weeks in the upper part of its 12 month range, it was tepid at the beginning of the year, before improving in the past two months, even making a new 12 month high before backing off. It has improved considerably along with nominal consumer spending in the past several months.

Transport

Railroads (from the AAR)

  • Carloads up +0.6% YoY (cumulative 2025 vs. 2024 up +2.5%)
  • Intermodal units up +1.2% YoY (cumulative up +3.1%)
  • Total loads up +0.9% YoY (cumulative up +2.4%).

(Graph at Railfax Report - North American Rail Freight Traffic Carloading Report.)

Shipping transport

  • Harpex up +3 to 2213 (new 24 month high)
  • Baltic Dry Index down -54 to 1963.

Rail was mixed for many weeks before turning positive in March, presumably reflecting businesses attempting to get inventory before the new tariffs kick in. Intermodal rail loads had steadily decelerated since then, and five weeks ago turned negative. The total number of total loads was negative for several weeks as well. All three measures improved sharply in July, and remained positive, but have weakened in the month.

Harpex was as high as nearly 4500 almost 2 years ago, but declined as low as 810 in December of last year before rising, setting repeated 12 month highs in the past four months. The usual interpretation for this is that demand is straining against supply, showing a strong global economy, and thus very positive.

Similarly, the BDI was in a generally declining trend throughout 2022 before bottoming at a three year low of 530 in February 2023. The overall trend has been higher since then, rising to a high of 3346 last December, probably reflecting the Houthi attacks on shipping in the Red Sea. This year its low was 1309 in January, and its recent high was 2419 last March. It declined to the bottom 1/3rd of its one year range during the rest of the year, but rebounded all the way to the top of its six month range earlier this year, before retreating back into the middle of that range.

I am wary of reading too much into price indexes like this, since they are heavily influenced by supply (as in, a huge overbuilding of ships in the last decade) as well as demand.

Steel production (American Iron and Steel Institute)

  • Down -0.6% w/w
  • Up +2.5% YoY.

This metric has been very volatile for over a year. Recently it improved to neutral and then positive for two weeks before backing off to neutral again in May. Last week its YoY measure was sufficiently small to change the rating to neutral, and seven weeks ago it returned to positive.

Summary And Conclusion

Below are this week’s spreadsheets of the long leading, short leading, and coincident readings. Check marks indicate the present reading. If there has been a change this week, the prior reading is marked with an X:

Long leading Indicators Positive Neutral Negative
Corporate bonds ✓
10 year Treasury ✓
10 yr-2 yr Treasury ✓
10 yr. - 3 mo. Treasury ✓
2 yr - Fed funds ✓
Mortgage rates ✓
Purchase Mtg. Apps. ✓
Refi Mtg Apps. ✓
Real Estate Loans ✓
Real M1 ✓
Real M2 ✓
Corporate Profits ✓
Adj. Fin. Conditions Index ✓
Leverage Index ✓
Totals: 6 3 5
Short Leading Indicators Positive Neutral Negative
Credit Spread ✓
Miller Score ✓
St. L. Fin. Stress Index ✓
US$ Broad ✓
US$ Major currencies ✓
Total commodities ✓
Industrial commodities ✓ X
Stock prices ✓
Regional Fed New Orders ✓
Initial jobless claims x ✓
Temporary staffing ✓
Gas prices ✓
Oil prices ✓
Gas Usage X ✓
Totals: 9 3 2
Coincident Indicators Positive Neutral Negative
Weekly Econ. Index ✓
Open Table ✓
Redbook ✓
Rail ✓ X
Harpex ✓
BDI ✓
Steel ✓
Tax Withholding ✓
TED & LIBOR (deleted)
SOFR ✓
Financial Cond. Index ✓
Totals: 9 1 0

.

The long leading indicators continue to be very mixed, with bond market related indicators being much more negative than monetary and corporate ones.

The short leading indicators remain very positive, but it is noteworthy that initial jobless claims turned back neutral. The coincident indicators remain very positive, paced by strong consumer spending.

The surprise improvement in the regional Fed indexes last month was one of those things that get noticed much sooner via the high frequency data vs. waiting for several monthly reports, and was reflected in the sharp improvement of the ISM indexes this past week.

I continue to watch consumer spending and initial jobless claims in particular like the proverbial hawk.

data.title: Ollie's Bargain Outlet Holdings: Long-Term Growth Story Remains Intact
data.description:
Investment action

I had a buy rating for Ollie’s Bargain Outlet Holdings (NASDAQ:OLLI) previously, as I continue to see a clear growth story playing out, with solid execution and healthy underlying demand. After reviewing the latest quarter, I stay buy-rated. OLLI is capitalizing aggressively on competitor exits, accelerating store expansion, and leveraging its loyalty program to drive transaction-led CSS. With a broader customer base, more younger shoppers coming into the ecosystem, and a strong balance sheet, I see the long-term growth story intact.

Earnings update

In 2Q25 net sales were $679.6 million, up 17.5% y/y, with comparable store sales [CSS] up 5% on higher transactions. Unit growth also contributed well with 29 openings in the quarter, ending at 613 stores across 34 states, up 16.8% y/y. Margins progressed well too. Gross profit was $271.3 million, and gross margin reached 39.9%, up 200 bps y/y, primarily from lower supply chain costs and a stronger merchandise margin. SG&A did step up to 25.8% on higher medical and casualty claims and slightly higher store labor, but operating income still came in at $77 million with operating margin expanded to 11.3% (up ~80 bps y/y), and net income was $61.3 million with diluted EPS of $0.99; adjusted EPS was also $0.99, up 26.9% y/y. Management then upgraded guidance, now expecting openings to be 85 (from 75), net sales to be $2.631–$2.644 billion, CSS to be 3%–3.5%, gross margin to be 40.3%, and adj. EPS to be $3.76–$3.84, all assuming current tariffs hold.

Broad-Based Demand and Resonating Value Proposition

Digging into the details, the quality of OLLI’s 5% y/y CSS growth makes the result even more encouraging. The noteworthy aspect here is that this growth was transaction-led, with the average basket size essentially flat. For a value retailer, I take this as the healthiest form of growth, as it reflects a broader customer base and increased visit frequency rather than simply leaning on higher pricing. CSS cadence intra-quarter was also very encouraging, with May flat, followed by momentum built in June, and July was strongest. Importantly, management confirmed that transaction growth has carried into August.

The other important takeaway I had was that OLLI's value proposition looks to be widening beyond the traditional core customer. Management highlighted strength in new shoppers from mid-to-upper income brackets, which is perhaps the clearest evidence of a “trade-down” effect in the current environment. At the same time, OLLI’s targeted digital marketing is working well at bringing younger consumers into its ecosystem—on the 2Q25 earnings call, management confirmed that OLLI’s customer file is getting younger in the existing customer base. This has very important long-term implications since it relates to the longevity of the business (i.e., long-term brand relevance since younger generations are eventually going to be the main bulk of consumers).

In my view, this dynamic where OLLI is capturing more affluent, value-seeking households while at the same time capturing more younger shoppers really strengthens OLLI’s growth outlook.

Accelerating Market Share Capture Through Stores and Loyalty

Drawing back to one of the points I made in my last post—the tailwind from competitor exits—OLLI is clearly capitalizing on this opportunity, and it is doing even stronger than what I had expected. We can see this from the pace of expansion in 2Q25, wherein OLLI opened 29 new stores in 2Q25 and 54 in 1H25 alone, which is already above its last full-year record. It makes a lot of sense for OLLI to accelerate expansion here since the faster OLLI moves to capture the white space, the faster it can secure market share. The momentum is not stopping. In the call, management raised its FY25 new store target to 85, which equates to a 15.2% y/y unit growth rate, implying another 31 stores to be opened in 2H25. Importantly, this is not just a one-off acceleration. Management has already suggested that another year of elevated openings is likely in 2026, which basically makes this a multi-year growth catalyst rather than a short-term land grab.

What makes this push to expand the store base more attractive than it looks is OLLI’s Army loyalty program. In 2Q25, membership grew 10.6% y/y to 16.1 million, with loyal customers accounting for >80% of sales (same as 1Q25), and the real development that got me bullish here is the revamp of the “Ollie’s Days” event into a member-exclusive promotion. This revamp was a major success, as it contributed ~100 bps to quarterly CSS growth while being accretive to earnings. In other words, the event drove both revenue growth and was profitable. To give a better sense of how successful this event was, consider that this June event surpassed even the traditional December Army Night, which historically has been the program’s largest event.

Clearly, the flywheel is now spinning faster. The aggressive new store growth is feeding Ollie’s Army, and a stronger Army is fueling the high-value traffic that drives the entire flywheel of more stores > more traffic > more members > more capital to open stores.

Balance sheet strength

OLLI’s has a “fortress” balance sheet to support this entire aggressive growth plan. The numbers say it all. As of the end of 2Q25, OLLI had $460.3 million in cash and investments, up 30.3% y/y, with no meaningful long-term debt. It also has an undrawn $100 million credit facility that adds another $90.4 million of available liquidity. This basically gives OLLI the firepower to fund its FY25 capex plan, which is budgeted at $83 to $88 million for 85 new store openings, while also continuing its share repurchase program, all without leverage. More importantly, I also think it provides good flexibility to capitalize on unique opportunities, like acquiring leases from bankrupt retailers or building inventory to lock in attractive deals.

Valuation

The upside is certainly lesser than when I wrote about this company back in June, but I still think it deserves a buy rating. I believe the strong growth momentum can continue into FY26 (I upgraded revenue growth in FY25/26 to 16%) before moderating back to 10%. And this should justify OLLI to continue trading at 30x forward PE (~4x higher than its 5-year average). I am not being aggressive to assume margin expansion, but I do see potential for that to happen since there is an inherent operating leverage in OLLI business model. At 30x, I have a price target of $148, which equates to a share price upside of 15%.

Risk

There are some risks to note. SG&A did grow faster than sales in 2Q25, up 60 bps y/y, driven by higher medical and casualty claims. Also, the store expansion plans also come with meaningful near-term costs, as pre-opening expenses surged 95.3% y/y. While these investments are necessary to support growth and market share capture, they weigh on near-term profitability. If sales are weaker than expected, it could really hit near-term earnings, which will not be pretty since OLLI is already trading at a premium to history.

Conclusion

In conclusion, I maintain my buy rating on OLLI. The momentum in CSS, transaction growth, and customer file health, coupled with record store openings and a powerful loyalty ecosystem, makes the long-term growth outlook remain attractive. Valuation is no longer as cheap at 30x forward PE, but I believe the premium is justified given the visibility of double-digit growth through FY27.

data.title: Buckle Up For The Biggest Market Disruption Since The Industrial Revolution
data.description:
Introduction

Earlier this week, I participated in the introductory lecture for the Purchasing Management course at my alma mater in the Netherlands. It's been about six years since I took that course, which was part of my master's degree in International Business Administration with a major in Purchasing & Supply Management.

I'm telling you this for two reasons. Reason one is that, although I never worked a day as an actual purchasing manager or supply chain specialist, I have always used these skills in my "Big Picture" research, as everything we do every day is about supply chains. Ever since the pandemic, even "the masses" have found out just how critical efficient supply chains are.

The other reason is that during the Q&A part of the lecture, someone asked me if I had advice for students who were about to enter the workforce within 12-15 months, which applied to nearly everyone in the room.

While I was flattered because this is usually a question someone should ask famous business people instead of me, I said to prepare for a massive AI-fueled disruption, which requires people to focus on finding unique skills that can ideally be complemented by AI but not erased.

That disruption is what I want to discuss today.

In recent months, I have spent a lot of time on issues like "higher for longer" inflation, the Trump administration's desire to "run it hot," where "it" refers to the economy, and how this comes with both opportunities and elevated risks.

One of these risks is that inflation is getting out of control, pressuring consumers without considerable assets to ease the pain. From a social and economic point of view, this is one of my biggest concerns.

We have also spent considerable time on artificial intelligence, as I am a big fan of investing in so-called "AI bottlenecks" like power, infrastructure, suppliers, and water, and because I am convinced that most people are still massively underestimating the power of AI and what this could mean for the economy, you, me, and everyone else.

So, as we have a lot of important developments to discuss, let's get right to it!

The Death Of The American Dream

What's the American dream?

To some, it's owning an AMG Mercedes and a vacation home in Naples, Florida.

Others consider it to be a situation where one is raising a family in a paid-off home in a safe suburban neighborhood with good schools, two cars, and the ability to go on vacation once or twice every year.

Interestingly enough, and using the words of the George W. Bush Institute, it's whatever you want it to be, as it's essentially the opportunity (equal for all) to achieve your dreams:

No less an authority than the Oxford English Dictionary defines the American dream as “the ideal that every citizen of the United States should have an equal opportunity to achieve success and prosperity through hard work, determination, and initiative.” - GWBI

Now, that dream seems to be under tremendous pressure, as The Wall Street Journal wrote in a recent exclusive piece titled "Americans Lose Faith That Hard Work Leads to Economic Gains, WSJ-NORC Poll Finds."

I think the first two paragraphs speak for themselves:

A new Wall Street Journal-NORC poll finds that the share of people who say they have a good chance of improving their standard of living fell to 25%, a record low in surveys dating to 1987. More than three-quarters said they lack confidence that life for the next generation will be better than their own, the poll found.

Nearly 70% of people said they believe the American dream—that if you work hard, you will get ahead—no longer holds true or never did, the highest level in nearly 15 years of surveys. - WSJ

The chart that goes with it is quite something:

Interestingly enough, one might make the case that consumers should be upbeat, as the predicted consumer sentiment shows. This indicator looked at unemployment, inflation, consumer spending, and the stock market.

However, the actual consumer sentiment number is horrible.

In order to explain the gap, one doesn't need a Ph.D. or an IQ of more than 120, as most of us know the economy consists of two camps, the haves and the have-nots.

So, let's quickly explain the gap between predicted and actual consumer sentiment:

  • Unemployment is indeed low. However, people do not base their expectations on current conditions but on what may be in the future. That's how our brains work. Right now, the fear is that AI is going to wreck many job opportunities. These fears are fully warranted, as most successful AI models are already smarter than humans. As we can see below, demand for jobs with high AI disruption risks has truly imploded. We also see that demand for young people has fallen, as people who come out of college often have only theoretical knowledge, something that AI competes with.

Between you and me, the two charts above worry me greatly.

  • Inflation is at 2.7% (all items). While it's 70 basis points above the Fed's target, it's not the worst number and definitely not something that screams the end of the American dream. However, we need to be aware that inflation has been above the Fed's target since 2021. Moreover, as I have often written, odds are that inflation remains higher for longer due to secular trends like the end of the U.S. shale revolution, deglobalization, tariffs, and massive AI spending.

I combined the next two items in one bullet point.

  • Consumer spending/stock market (wealth) are huge drivers of the American dream. Unfortunately, the stock market's all-time high has mainly benefited the people with substantial assets. The same goes for the housing market, which is a great wealth builder. Because of elevated home prices and high-interest rates, housing affordability has fallen off a cliff.

In other words, people are fearing for their jobs, unable to buy a house, and not really benefiting from the stock market's massive gains.

All of this may soon get worse.

Buckle Up, AI Is About To Disrupt

Have you heard of Leopold Aschenbrenner?

He's a 23-year-old German-born AI "influencer" who graduated from Columbia as valedictorian at 19 with triple degrees in math, stats, and economics.

Now, he runs a multi-billion-dollar fund in San Francisco called "Situational Awareness," which is named after his AI thesis. Essentially, he is investing with a similar strategy to mine, as he invests in global stocks that benefit from the development of AI, including semiconductors, infrastructure, and power companies. He's shorting companies in industries that may be disrupted.

In the first six months of this year, he made 47%.

Investments include the semiconductor VanEck ETF (SMH), Broadcom (AVGO), Intel (INTC), Vistra Corp. (VST), Core Scientific (CORZ), EQT Corp. (EQT), Constellation Energy Corp. (CEG), Applied Digital Corp. (APLD), and more.

The reason I'm bringing all of this up is because Aschenbrenner predicts an "intelligence explosion" where AI automates AI research, compressing decades of progress into years. He frames this as a national security imperative for the U.S. and warns of risks from authoritarian regimes like China, espionage, misalignment, and weaponization.

You can read his full essay here.

Aschenbrenner, who is way smarter than me on this subject, brings up one thing I have said over and over again, which is that AI will become much more powerful than one can imagine, as it could fit decades of algorithmic progress into just one year, which would massively accelerate innovation beyond anything we can currently comprehend.

Needless to say, this includes worker replacement, as AI models are already smarter than most people. For now, we're lucky that AI systems do not function as standalone applications. At least in most business settings.

Especially as we enter the age of superintelligence, which is nothing compared to the GPT-4 capabilities we currently enjoy, the disruption really starts to take off, likely soaring somewhere between 2026 and 2028.

As one can imagine, this could lead to mass unemployment in cognitive sectors, although he notes a potential "long tail" for full automation and initial human oversight. Once we are further in the superintelligence phase, dealing with AI will be like dealing with an actual human being.

By the end of this, I expect us to get something that looks a lot like a drop-in remote worker. An agent that joins your company, is onboarded like a new human hire, messages you and colleagues on Slack and uses your softwares, makes pull requests, and that, given big projects, can do the model-equivalent of a human going away for weeks to independently complete the project. - Situational Awareness

Moreover, he calls for an industrial revolution, as industrial sectors are often constrained by labor. Currently, that's one of the reasons why many large projects are slow to get off the ground. Once these labor bottlenecks are slowly solved by robotics and mass automation, we could see a massive CapEx boom and a widening physical footprint of many companies. That's on top of hyper-fast innovation in areas like healthcare, computing, and anything else that AI can rapidly accelerate.

For companies, disruption means adaptation or extinction. As straightforward as that may sound, it's what we are dealing with. According to the report, trillion-dollar cluster buildouts create winners (e.g., AI leaders with $10T valuations), but automation forces workflow changes and potential government mergers to limit private control.

Painting with a broad brush, here are some of the things we could encounter when it comes to disruption:

  • Pharmaceutical companies could use AI to develop new drugs within weeks, often tailoring drugs to individual needs, something that current drugs are not capable of (and why many fail).
  • We could disrupt the entertainment industry by simply asking AI to generate shows, movies, and games for us. All tailored to our desires.
  • Trucks, cars, and taxis could all become fully remote, limiting self-driving cars to tracks. The same happened to horses.
  • Finance and banking could become fully decentralized.
  • We could all use AI to create websites and complex programs within minutes.

What does this mean for car companies, consumer brands, industrial companies, healthcare, defense, government organizations, and so much more?

I don't have the answer, but I know for sure that disruption risks have not been this great since the start of the Industrial Revolution, especially when it comes to labor market risks. While the Internet did a number on many companies that were unprepared, it was never a major risk for the job market.

On top of that, I also believe every single investor is required to check their portfolios line by line and check for disruption risks. Although we don't know yet how powerful AI could become, we should approach this with some creativity and assess the pros and cons of AI on every investment we have.

It also explains why investors are paying 22.5x forward earnings for the S&P 500. As the market now expects the biggest surge in margins in decades due to the power of AI, there really isn't a good alternative to American stocks, especially if we consider that the nation is the current AI leader.

However, in order to maintain its leading position, the country needs to address critical challenges like power access and grid modernization. It also has to tackle social issues like employment support while implementing new measures to stay ahead in global defense.

After all, we can assume the widespread adoption of hyper-intelligent AI by other nations could increase the risk of weapons of mass destruction, as highlighted in the Social Awareness paper.

So many issues and not enough answers, which is why I stick to my strategy:

  • Focus on ultra-wide moat companies that have competitive advantages that are unlikely to be broken by AI. It's hard to assess, but we have to give it a shot.
  • While I expect market strength to broaden and the equal-weight S&P 500 to outperform the market-weighted (the "normal") S&P 500, I believe the current market valuation needs to be taken with a grain of salt.
  • I am putting more money to work in the AI supply chain soon. This includes infrastructure, power, midstream, and others.
  • I am prepared to focus a lot more on Big Picture issues tied to AI, as I believe social and geopolitical issues will rapidly evolve in the years ahead.

That said, as serious as all of this is, I'm very upbeat about the future and believe if we put some energy into (literally and figuratively), we'll be just fine.

This goes for people who are about to enter the workforce soon and retirees wondering what AI disruption may mean for their dividend income.

For now, however, this is my takeaway:

Takeaway

My takeaway is that we are in the early innings of the most significant economic disruption since the Industrial Revolution, fully fueled by AI.

The American Dream is already under pressure from inflation and a terrifying gap between the haves and have-nots.

On top of that, an intelligence explosion is coming that could compress decades of progress into just a few years. As dramatic as all of this may sound, I think it's truly happening. Also, it's a national security risk with substantial social consequences on top of an economic earthquake.

For investors, this means we are required to check every line of our portfolios for disruption risks while trying to capitalize on AI without buying into small startups that won't likely survive. To me, this means buying power, infrastructure, and non-AI companies that have the ability to implement it.

For students entering the workforce, my only advice is to find a unique skill that can be complemented by AI. I would also suggest investing every penny in the stock market to build wealth, as I think job security is a thing of the past. At least for most white collar workers, including me.

That said, while the path ahead is uncertain, I'm not worried.

As long as we know what's going on, adapt accordingly, and don't forget to enjoy every day we're alive, I think we'll all be just fine.

(Don't worry, be happy)
